data.title: Healthcare Triangle Shares Surge on AI Agent Launch
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Shares of Healthcare Triangle surged after the company launched an artificial-intelligence agent for customer engagement.

Shares were up 36% to $3.98 in after-hours trading on Wednesday, after closing up 11% at $2.92. At the close, the stock had fallen 98% over the past six months.

The healthcare and life sciences technology company said Wednesday that it deployed an agentic AI tool on Teyame AI's customer engagement platform. Healthcare Triangle inked a deal in January to acquire Teyame AI, a Spain-based AI customer service company.

It said Wednesday that it hopes to scale the agentic AI capabilities across the U.S. and Latin America following Teyame's success in Spain.

The AI agent is able to automate human-like customer service conversations at scale, with security and regulatory compliance built in, the company said.

"I am excited that HCTI is at the cutting edge of Agentic.AI integration and will be able to roll it out across multiple languages in new geographies and accelerate our growth over next few years," Healthcare Triangle Chief Financial Officer David Ayanoglou said.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Arm's stock shoots to best day in a year as an Nvidia-like chapter may be starting
data.description:
By Hannah Pedone

Arm could dominate the CPU market the way Nvidia has come to dominate the GPU market, an analyst says

Arm announced on Tuesday that it will sell its own CPU.

Arm Holdings could be on track for a Nvidia-like ascent, according to an analyst, as the company gets ready to sell its first chip.

Until now, Arm (ARM) has largely derived its revenue from royalties and from licensing its intellectual property to chip designers. But on Tuesday, the company announced the launch of its own central processing unit, which it says is meant to support agentic artificial intelligence.

While Arm's stock fell 1.4% on Tuesday after the announcement, it surged 16.4% in Wednesday trading as analysts weighed in on the implications of Arm's CPU efforts - both in terms of the company's narrative and its financial prospects. The stock made its biggest one-day gain since it rose 24.2% on April 9, 2025, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Evercore ISI analyst Mark Lipacis said that Arm's story reminds him of Nvidia's (NVDA). Nvidia's graphics processing units have been the backbone of AI development thus far, but central processing units are having a moment. Lipacis said Arm's energy-efficient architecture and other aspects of its chip development lend themselves well to the demands of current agentic AI workflows.

Lipacis noted that the difference between Arm and Nvidia, however, is that Nvidia developed its chips and then "fought to become the de facto standard" for GPUs. By contrast, he said, Arm's underlying technology is already the de facto standard for CPUs. Now the company is just delivering its own chips based on that standard, which Lipacis called the "easier part."

He increased his price target to $227 from $170 in his latest note to clients.

See also: Micron's stock is spectacularly cheap, as these numbers show

Meanwhile, Raymond James analyst Simon Leopold upgraded Arm's stock to outperform from perform, writing that he had been hoping the company would shift its business model in this way, but was uncertain whether Arm would actually move to start selling its own chips.

The decision could have big implications for Arm's financials, he noted, citing the company's projections. Arm expects about $15 billion of revenue from the chip business in fiscal 2031, for instance. Leopold said that the new initiative could help offset slowing growth in the market for Arm's smartphone chip designs.

He also noted that Meta (META) will be a major customer for the new chip, with plans to use the CPU in its data-center build-out.

TD Cowen's Krish Sankar said Arm may just be getting started in its journey as a seller of chips. He said the company has "other categories of chips in the works," such as those for autonomous vehicles and robotics.

Read more: Arm's stock could rocket 50% as Wall Street wakes up to a 'game-changing' trend, analyst says

-Hannah Pedone

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: How ~$30k in Liquidity Supported $4m in 30-Day Trading Volume
data.description:
By Jamie McCormick, Co-CMO, Stabull Labs

The eleventh article in the 15 part “Deconstructing DeFi” Series.

⸻

At the time of writing, one Stabull pool held approximately $31,000 in liquidity.

Over the preceding 30 days, that same pool supported around $4.05 million in trading volume.

For anyone used to judging decentralised exchanges by raw TVL alone, that ratio looks unusual. But it makes sense once you understand how liquidity is actually being used inside modern DeFi execution flows.

Why TVL and volume are often misunderstood

In much of DeFi, TVL is treated as a proxy for success.

Higher TVL is assumed to mean deeper liquidity, better execution, and more trading activity. In practice, TVL only tells you how much capital is sitting in a pool. It tells you very little about how often that capital is used.

A large pool with little interaction can generate minimal fees. A smaller pool that sits on active execution paths can generate meaningful volume again and again.

The pool we examined falls squarely into the second category.

Parked liquidity vs working liquidity

The key distinction is not size, but utilisation.

In the transactions we traced, this pool was rarely the destination. Instead, it functioned as:

  • a stable execution leg
  • an FX-anchored pricing step
  • a reliable mid-path conversion

Each time a trade passed through, only a small portion of the pool was used. But those interactions happened continuously.

Over the course of a month, those small, repeatable interactions added up to $1.85 million in total volume.

Where the yield actually comes from

A common question — especially from people newer to DeFi — is how platforms can offer yield on stablecoin deposits at all.

In many protocols, the answer is lending. Assets are deposited, lent out to borrowers, and interest is generated from repayment over time. That model can work, but it introduces credit risk, liquidation risk, and reliance on borrower solvency.

Stabull works differently.

Liquidity provision on Stabull is closer to a toll booth than a lending desk.

LPs are not lending assets to be repaid later. They are providing liquidity that sits directly on execution paths. Every time a transaction flows through a pool, a small toll is paid.

No repayment risk. No leverage. Yield comes directly from usage.

The maths in practice

Using the example we analysed:

  • Pool TVL: ~$31,000
  • Trading volume (30 days): $4.05 million
  • Swap fee: 0.015%
  • LP share of fees: 70%

Fees generated (30 days)

$4,050,000 × 0.015% = $607.50 in total swap fees

LPs receive 70% of this:

$604.50 × 70% = $425.25 paid to LPs over the 30-day period

These fees are paid in the output currency of each swap, meaning LPs earn yield directly in liquid stablecoin assets.

Annualised view (illustrative)

If this level of activity were sustained over a full year (an assumption, not a guarantee):

$194.25 × 12 = $2,331 in annualised LP fees

On ~$31,000 of TVL, that equates to:

~6.7% annualised return from swap fees alone

This figure:

  • comes purely from transaction activity
  • does not rely on lending or leverage
  • is generated in liquid assets

Actual returns vary with volume and TVL, but the example illustrates how usage translates into yield.

Why execution quality matters more than depth

Execution systems — bots, solvers, and aggregators — do not route trades based on pool size alone.

They care about:

  • predictable pricing
  • low slippage for the trade size
  • reliability inside atomic execution
  • failure risk

Because Stabull uses oracle-anchored pricing, execution through the pool remains stable even as external markets move. That makes it suitable for repeated use inside automated strategies.

As a result, the pool was selected again and again — not because it was the largest, but because it was dependable.

Fees accumulate quietly, but consistently

There was no single large trade responsible for most of the volume.

Instead, activity came from:

  • frequent, smaller swaps
  • automated execution
  • non-UI flows

Each interaction paid swap fees. Over time, those fees accumulated steadily.

This contrasts sharply with incentive-driven volume, which often arrives in bursts and disappears just as quickly.

Yield beyond swap fees

Swap fees are only one part of the picture.

In addition, LPs on Stabull may receive STABUL token rewards via the Liquidity Mining Program run through Merkl.

These incentives are separate from swap fees and can:

  • supplement organic yield
  • attract additional liquidity
  • be held, sold, or used to participate in protocol governance

Swap fees reflect real usage. Incentives are designed to accelerate growth.

Why this pattern is durable

Nothing about this case relied on special conditions.

There was:

  • no liquidity mining campaign driving usage
  • no marketing push
  • no exclusive partnership

The pool was simply useful.

As long as execution systems need stable, FX-anchored pricing, the pool remains relevant. As overall DeFi activity increases, the number of times those execution paths are triggered increases too.

That is how relatively modest liquidity can support disproportionate volume.

What this tells us about Stabull’s role

Stabull pools are not competing to be the deepest venue for speculative trading. They are competing to be:

  • reliable
  • predictable
  • easy to integrate into automated execution

When a pool succeeds at that, volume becomes a function of ecosystem activity rather than direct user acquisition.

Looking ahead

As more execution systems, aggregators, and protocols interact with Stabull, this pattern is likely to repeat across other pools.

Liquidity does not need to be enormous to be effective. It needs to be usable.

In the next article, we’ll turn the focus explicitly toward liquidity providers and unpack what LPs are actually getting paid for on Stabull — and why non-UI volume often represents higher-quality fee generation than traditional retail trading.

About the Author

Jamie McCormick is Co-Chief Marketing Officer at Stabull Finance, where he has been working for over two years on positioning the protocol within the evolving DeFi ecosystem.

He is also the founder of Bitcoin Marketing Team, established in 2014 and recognised as Europe’s oldest specialist crypto marketing agency. Over the past decade, the agency has worked with a wide range of projects across the digital asset and Web3 landscape.

Jamie first became involved in crypto in 2013 and has a long-standing interest in Bitcoin and Ethereum. Over the last two years, his focus has increasingly shifted toward understanding the mechanics of decentralised finance, particularly how on-chain infrastructure is used in practice rather than in theory.

data.title: Kennedy allies petition to broaden US vaccine injury list
data.description:
Key points:
  • Group wants over 300 vaccine injury conditions added to list
  • ICAN, founded by Kennedy ally, files petition with HHS
  • Threatens lawsuit if rulemaking doesn't begin in 60 days
  • Kennedy, a longtime anti-vaccine activist, has criticized the program

By Ahmed Aboulenein and Julie Steenhuysen

An anti-vaccine group aligned with U.S. Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is petitioning him to vastly broaden a federal list to include more than 300 injuries it says are linked to immunizations, and has threatened to sue if he does not.

The petition, dated March 20, calls for amendments to the Vaccine Injury Table, which currently lists 47 potential vaccine-related injuries. It enables expedited compensation for claimants without proving a vaccine actually caused their health issue if their symptoms match those on the list.

The petition includes a 60-day notice of intent to sue if Kennedy does not begin the process of amending the table.

The Informed Consent Action Network, part of the Kennedy-led Make America Healthy Again movement and founded by Kennedy's former presidential campaign communications director Del Bigtree, based its petition on government-commissioned reports that evaluated adverse events associated with vaccines.

The reports did not establish vaccine causation for most injuries, but the petition argues that the government's commissioning of the studies, in which it uses the word "associated" or says there is "some basis" to believe there is a causal link suffices under the legal standard for adding to the table, which says injuries "associated" with vaccines.

CRITICISM OF PETITION

Scientific and legal experts said the petition stretched legal definitions and would hamper future government research into vaccine injuries.

Noel Brewer, a public health professor at the University of North Carolina and a former member of a U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's independent panel of outside vaccine experts, dismissed the reasoning as flawed.

"The lawyer is making the argument that the government considering if something could be harmful made it a harm that should be memorialized as such, and that doesn't make any sense," he said. Brewer was among 17 panel members fired by Kennedy last year.

Dorit Reiss, a vaccine law expert at the University of California College of the Law, San Francisco, also criticized the petition.

"The petition argues that simply analyzing potential harms establishes an association, even if the government found no evidence of one. That interpretation stretches the legal definitions," she said.

The White House and Department of Health and Human Services did not respond to requests for comment.

The Vaccine Injury Compensation Program, a no-fault alternative created to protect vaccine makers and providers from costly litigation and ensure vaccine supply stability, allows claimants to file injury claims against the government, not manufacturers.

LONG-HELD AMBITION

The petition places Kennedy, who profited from his anti-vaccine activities before becoming HHS Secretary, in a position to act on a long-held policy goal to reform the compensation system. He has previously described it as "broken" and "a morass of inefficiency."

In August, Kennedy reinstated a federal task force for safer childhood vaccines that had been inactive for nearly three decades, one day before a deadline to respond to a lawsuit funded by Children's Health Defense, an anti-vaccine group that he founded.

ICAN's lawyer Aaron Siri, who has worked with Kennedy on vaccine litigation in the past and served as his presidential campaign's lawyer, was asked whether he discussed the petition with the health secretary prior to filing it.

"This petition and notice was submitted without any coordination with anyone inside the federal government and we hope that HHS does the right thing in response to it," Siri told Reuters.

TENSIONS WITH TRUMP ADMINISTRATION

The move is the latest sign of tensions between Kennedy's MAHA base and President Donald Trump's administration. Trump angered Kennedy's supporters earlier this year when he signed an executive order to boost domestic production of the weedkiller glyphosate, which they oppose.

Siri, in a post on X on Monday, suggested that political constraints could hinder Kennedy's ability to act.

"I have no doubt RFK Jr. wants to update the table and the only reason he wouldn't is because the White House won't let him," Siri wrote. "If that happens, a federal lawsuit will be forthcoming at the end of the 60-day notice period."

data.title: MillerKnoll: Middle East Conflict Will Hurt Shipments, Raise Costs — Market Talk
data.description: MillerKnoll says the conflict in the Middle East will lead to minimal shipments to the region and drive up logistics costs in the current quarter, hurting results. "In the near term, the current conflict is creating disruption and we do expect some impact to fourth-quarter sales and costs," Chief Executive Andi Owen says during a call with analysts. Owen says the region remains an important long-term growth opportunity for MillerKnoll's international contract business. The office-furniture company's guidance for the fiscal fourth quarter factors in a headwind of about $8 million to $9 million, or 9 cents to 10 cents a share, tied to the effects of the conflict. The midpoints of the company's forecast missed analyst expectations. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Tron 2025 10-K: Revenue $4.74M, EPS (loss) $0.16
data.description:
Tron reported fiscal 2025 revenue of $4.74 million and a basic loss per share of $0.16, with operating losses narrowing year-over-year despite a larger net loss driven by digital asset fair value effects. Revenue rose modestly from $4.31 million in 2024 while gross profit improved, but net loss widened to $(16.811) million largely from non-operational digital-asset adjustments. The company highlights continued brand repositioning and a digital asset treasury strategy as part of its operational focus.

Financial Highlights

  • Sales (Revenue): $4.741 million, up from $4.311 million year-over-year.
  • Gross Profit: $1.198 million, increased from $0.855 million year-over-year.
  • Operating loss: $(2.518) million, improved versus $(4.335) million prior year.
  • Net Income (Loss): $(16.811) million, compared to $(4.339) million prior year (includes large digital asset fair value effects).
  • Basic (loss) per share: $(0.16), vs. $(0.37) prior year.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue growth context: Revenue modestly rose to $4.74M in 2025 from $4.31M in 2024, reflecting stable sales amid theme-park order timing shifts.
  • Channel & product mix: Continued focus on licensed toys and souvenirs sold to major parks such as Disney, Universal and Six Flags with SKU prices generally ranging from $2.50 to $50.
  • Brand momentum: Rebranded to align with the TRON ecosystem; ticker change and strategic investments to bolster crypto-focused identity and visibility.
  • Digital asset strategy: Built a digital asset treasury and staked nearly all TRX holdings into sTRX via JustLend to generate yield and long-term value.
  • Production & supply model: Operates a low-fixed-cost, agile production model using global third-party manufacturers to enable quick product launches tied to pop‑culture trends.

Original SEC Filing: Tron Inc. [ TRON ] - 10-K - Mar. 25, 2026

data.title: Health In Tech posts $33.3M revenue, $1.28M net income in 10-K
data.description:
Health In Tech reported fiscal 2025 revenues of $33.33 million, a 71.0% increase year-over-year, and net income of $1.28 million (net margin 3.8%), reflecting significant top-line expansion alongside margin pressure. Gross profit was $20.94 million with a 62.8% margin, down from 79.2% the prior year due to higher cost of revenues. The company reported operating income of $1.53 million and noted adjusted EBITDA improvement to $4.11 million driven by operating leverage.

Financial Highlights

  • Total revenues: $33.33 million, up 71.0% from $19.49 million in 2024.
  • Gross profit: $20.94 million; gross margin 62.8% (down from 79.2% in 2024).
  • Operating income: $1.53 million.
  • Net income: $1.28 million; net margin 3.8%, up from $0.67 million in 2024.
  • Net income per share: Omitted per source instructions.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue growth drivers: 71% YoY revenue growth driven by expanded channel adoption and larger employer clients.
  • Channel strategy: Continued reliance on brokers, TPAs and agencies; SMR fee revenues grew 106% as channel partnerships scaled.
  • Product and technology: Expanded AI-driven eDIYBS platform and completed a large-employer system expansion in September 2025; capitalized $3.47 million in software investment.
  • Partnerships and talent: Hosted hitDavos InsurTech Summit 2026, engaged AWS partner Ciklum, and appointed senior technology and growth leaders to scale the platform.
  • Operational efficiency: AR automation reduced turnover from 29 to 14 days; adjusted EBITDA improved to $4.11 million, indicating operating leverage.

Original SEC Filing: Health In Tech, Inc. [ HIT ] - 10-K - Mar. 25, 2026

data.title: Miran: AI and Deregulation Are Positive Supply Shocks To Economy — Market Talk
data.description: Fed governor Miran says that although the economy has faced negative supply shocks (such as the recent oil price spike) there have been positive ones recently too, such as AI and deregulation. "AI is a positive supply shock...It increases the production capacity of the economy," he said at the Digital Asset Summit in New York. Addressing the audience focused on crypto regulations, Miran says that having to deal with regulations has probably created obstacles for a number of people. "As those regulatory barriers recede, it becomes easier for you to make products that people want to buy," he said. (jessica.coacci@wsj.com; @jessica_coacci)
